post_rendered:
Four days ago. Road to Anthelios Prison.

Endless squadrons of grey clouds rolled across the darkening sky. Behind, the rumbling of the truck over gravel resounded in the empty trailer like constant thunder. Before, the last half hour's stretch of road snaked up and down over the bare, dusty hills.

The guide drove like he spoke: mechanically and monotonously. When he switched lanes, he always did so at the same calculated angle; when he proceeded down the same lane, there were no constant directional adjustments. And the guide never faltered, never tired. HannahW and mathjay, on the other hand, had long since settled for the couch in the sleeper cab. It was stiff and not particularly comfortable, but it was still a couch. From there Hannah could sit cross-legged while still more or less watching the directions they were going.

As they bumped into a particularly treacherous tract of mini boulders at the base of another hill, Hannah nearly tumbled off her seat. It was a small offense, but immediately her brain began to mentally list all the inconveniences and troubles they had run into as unpaid interns: pointless paperwork, workladen afterlifes, missing cupcakes, being stuck in a truck cab for hours on end. Steadying herself by grabbing the fold-out table, she sighed, but it was not all resigned forbearance. If work was a constant, dead or alive, as an unpaid intern, then so were its reliefs. Alive, it had been dumpling breaks, and, dead, it had been the sunrises, the conversations, the absence of the need to sleep. She had even flown a few times.

All in all, she made no complaints. If she did, she would see no end of The BossTM.

But this was the oddest scenario thus far. From beginning to the current moment, it was steeped with murky waters and plastered with silently screaming red flags. There was no normal here to start with. The destruction had already begun.

The prison loomed in view when Hannah looked up again from her reverie. She stood up unsteadily and attempted to stretch her legs.

. . .

Half an hour later. Anthelios Prison.

Nine prisoners lined up against the grey wall. They seemed only freshly groomed and recently washed up and stood, or, rather, slouched, blinking wearily at the dim light like teenagers at five or six in the morning.

HannahW perused them disapprovingly. It seemed like another uncooperative squad of underlings, but at least they did not seem inclined to cause trouble--at the moment, that was. Then Hannah suddenly remembered her question that she had intended to ask mathjay earlier but had forgotten. However, he was in a slouching state much like the convicts'. Three hours on the road rarely fails to gift disheveledness and mild stupor to passengers.

"These are the nine He requested," the darkly clothed guide flatly pronounced.

"Yes, yes," a man--probably the jailer--dressed in blue replied, waving a piece of paper grandly.

The guide neatly snatched the paper from the man and paused for a moment to consider it. Hannah was standing several feet from them and could not distinguish the paper's contents, but it was probably a list since a moment later the guide indicated something in the mid-lower region of the paper.

"These two must be right. He specifically requested," the guide recited.

The man in blue nodded twice as if to indicate two prisoners, but Hannah could scarcely tell which way the pudgy double chin was supposed to point. It quite literally had no point.

The guide did not look up but merely stated, "His requirements will be met." And he gestured to the man in blue to start leading the prisoners into the truck trailer.

Wait. His? Who? Only then did Hannah notice what had been said. Probably it had to do with whomever the employer was this time. However, she could not stand and think. Already the last of the others were vacating the room, and she strode after them.

As she was walking, she remembered her question again. She could perhaps ask mathjay on the ride back. She also had another question now.

. . .

Now. Floor 160, somewhere.

Slowly but surely nine has dwindled to three. Three of you. One is the killer. Just three days ago when you woke up to find the elevator in acrid ashes and supervisors dead, you had felt it surreal. Then it had become less so. And now it was surreal again.

Nine of you stood against that wall. Nine of you rode the elevator for the last time. Only two, if any of you will ever get down if anyone remembers you. And only two will ever make their way back to prison if you even live to make it back or if you even want to go back.


ad333 has perished, but not wholly been lost. There are rumors of his abilities reaching the ears of The BossTM, but results remain to be seen. Maybe he will be lauded for his valiant efforts if The BossTM is in a good mood, which has yet to happen. He was an Involuntarily Consummate Nutritionist (Town Cop).

post_rendered:
Wp tikki.

One particular thing I want to clear up: NKA is a real thing.

Yes, NKA is WIFOM. Every single non-mechanical thing in mafia is WIFOM. No, seriously. Name them all, line them all up. There's always some way scum can behave to greatly reduce the effectiveness of any tell or method.

Activity tells? Scum can just be active. Overreaction? Scum can avoid overreacting. Contribution? Scum can contribute.

NKA is real and useful, and the fact that you can slap a WIFOM label on something doesn't invalidate it on its own. WIFOM kills are rare. It's not about possibility, it's about probability.

The main problem that NKA usually runs into isn't the possibility of WIFOM, but the sheer number of explanations a kill can have. The killed player could:
  • Be seen as impossible to mishang
  • Have good reads
  • Have thread presence
  • Have a strong town reputation
  • Be a player that scum thought was a PR
  • Be a player that scum didn't think a protective role would target
  • An intentionally confusing kill made by well-positioned scum without high priority targets
  • Many, many others
Finding the exact correct choice out of all of these is what's so difficult.

If you can narrow down the possibilities reasonably well enough, though, NKA away.

^^
For example: "aroodman died because tikki was scared of being SRed" is bad NK analysis because aroodman has a strong thread presence, is experienced, is very well townread, can't be mishung, and any scum with brains kills him N1.

NKA that goes directly into reads and WIFOMy stuff such as "snoozing was killed go tikki could WIFOM" is bad

The trick here—and with everything—is to try to think from a scum POV rather than come up with reasons something could have happened.

post_rendered:
Four days ago. Road from Anthelios Prison.

"Who are these people?"

The trailer of the truck had been furnished. That had surprised you, but not much. You really didn't care. However, when two tall figures emerged from the far, shadowy corners of the truck, you had started. Who in their right mind would choose to stay in the pitch blackness of the back of a truck with the doors shut, you didn't know nor care. What sparked your dormant curiosity was the immense blankness of expression--or perhaps it was such immense complexity of expression that you could only call it blankness. Nevertheless, you were too apathetic to care beyond a minute or so after they shut the doors.

The two figures, guards, would not kill you, so went to sleep. And you had a dream. Your second to last one.

. . .

Now. Head Office, Floor 1, Avarice Spire.

"... who is he?"

He flicked the switch off, and the recorder ceased.

"Who am I?" the man slammed the device lightly onto the table and stood up. "Apparently someone who can't ever get his creations to listen," he shrugged as he strode past the stiff row of staff. "At least the Raven is doing his job. This is a wonderful success." He spun on his heel and started back across the length of the room. "And I'll have to work on my villain impression."

There was in fact little reason for him to be incensed at his staff. Their mistakes had turned out either well or of no consequence. He also understood that there were bound to be tradeoffs in the design of replicants and supposed there was no perfect balance for all situations. The price of human creativity was rebellion, and the price of mechanical obedience was inadaptability.

Take tikkitamatoa, his only remaining Raven… tikkitamatoa's generation had been essentially human. Then, he had focused on "nurture," ingraining desired values, forging obedient loyalty, and training applicable skills in the replicants from an exceedingly young age. In his early trials he had not been so skeptical of human caprice, for his only reference then was the first generation--one long since labelled an immense outlier. As soon as the squad of ten was sent out, it dissolved and none returned.

His best conjecture was that trauma had somehow completely wiped their memories of Avarice Lab (it had not been a skyscraper then) and its second floor. Nevertheless, there was one success: although the second generation's allegiance dissipated, its warped worldview persisted. He could rest in comfortable assurance that they would be criminals and labelled psychopaths for the remainder of their lives. Thus satisfied, he had dubbed the second generation "Ravens" and moved on.

The current staff were his only real success since then. Generation 159, completed a few years ago and thought near perfect at the time. They obeyed unquestioningly while, in simple terms, remaining "human" enough to carry out his business for himself. He had set them over the 160th floor last year, and it had gone well until the entire batch became deranged partway through. "Sheep" was his nickname for that generation.

A minor error early on when one of the 159th had briefly muddled his instructions was the cause of the derailment and ruin of the Sheep. And after listening to the assigned 159th guide's muddling in the Auditorium to HannahW, he figured that somehow and somewhere there was an issue of simplicity of mind--something he might parse out later after his plan unfolded.

At any rate, at the time of the catastrophe, he could not spare any of the 159th to clear the mess and had thus stalled. Until he discovered prison labor. And he discovered that two Ravens were in the local prison. And he discovered that bribes and status ironically worked very well with prison authorities.

Discreetly drafting two unpaid interns, he had thus begun the Avaritial experiment.

. . .

An hour later. Head Office, Floor 1, Avarice Spire.

"Welcome, tikkitamatoa," said the man from his comfortably tipped chair.

The office was simple. Plain whitish walls, generically carpeted floors, heavy wooden desk--only the office chair was elaborate: it had pockets and holders and buttons and paddings and all sorts of fancy but elegant gizmos, including but not limited to a sunglasses pouch and a mini hat rack.

tikkitamatoa looked confused and did not reply.

"I guess not, but congratulations. You've proved yourself, earned your freedom, and made the Avaritial ExperimentTM a great success--Aside from killing those interns, which I suspected would happen anyway. Typical collateral damage, don't worry. Perhaps you're willing to let me hire you to do a few more killings? Good. We can settle the details later. Try to not explode anything in the meantime."

Left alone again, the man spun around on his office chair thrice, snatched up his hat, slipped into his black trenchcoat, and smugly stuck on his sunglasses. And he strode out of the office to his limo which he drove to Unpaid Intern CentralTM just in case HannahW and mathjay revived early which never happened, but he did so anyway. Being The BossTM was more fun. It involved more ordering of people around. Being a double agent against your own organization for your own organization was also fun.


Rip. Felicityhe was an Incarcerated Janitor (Vanilla Townie).

The mafia faction has reached parity with town. Consequently, the game is now over.
Congratulations to bigmath_pro18 and tikkitamatoa for winning the game. The Mafia team has won.

Day 1

And so we arrive at Day 1, 600 posts in. Just know that no matter how long it took you to find the confirm rule, you can’t beat andy’s record:



In case you’re wondering, that’s 2 months, 25 days, 7 hours, and 1 minute.

ad333 and aroodman stopping the Felicityhe wagon in its tracks was beautiful to watch and also this:
aroodman #802 wrote:
Alright here it is.

If I haven't made it clear enough already, my PoE is {Felicity, HW, ostrich, tikki}.

There are three teams in this PoE that wouldn't include HW:
  • Felicity/ostrich
  • Felicity/tikki
  • ostrich/tikki
Felicity/ostrich is the one of these that I'm most willing to consider, but ostrich's vote comes after a game of mindless one-dimensional bloodthirst. Don't really think those are ingredients for a bus.
Felicity/tikki looked plausible up until tikki dropped his anti-DADV bit. tikki significantly decreased Felicity's chance of survival, intentionally. tikki's in a scarily small PoE. Not a good idea to bus D1 like that, and tikki would know this.
ostrich/tikki makes no sense for obvious reasons.

So yeah, that's a lot of assumptions, but if they're all true, you're scum.
was a beautiful example of scumteam-based analysis. It’s not the strongest reason for reads, but something you can check is what the possible scumteams are for people you scumread. If you don’t like any of the possible scumteams, you can use that as a reminder to maybe reconsider your reads! Oh yea, but also just listing possible scumteams doesn’t really do anything, please don’t do that, man I don’t know what I’m talking about aren’t I. I also personally think saying “that was a v/v interaction” or v/w or any of that isn’t very accurate without flips but what do I know?

...I just realized that feedback is me mainly pointing out that what aroodman is doing is what y’all should be doing but then also aroodman told me that he super tryharded D1 so he’d get NKed so... Also one of the things that aroodman really did was being proactive and interrogating people? Like, reading doesn’t have to be passive, you can and should actively go after the people you are unsure about and terrify them ask them questions until you can more satisfactarily resolve their slot. Legend.



Day 2
tikkitamatoa, #1142 wrote:
ad, I agree with a little bit of your reasoning but nightkill speculation is entirely WIFOM. Meaning: someone else could go “if we kill aroodman it’ll look bad on bmp and we can get him hanged”. Basing reads off of the reads of the dead person is called a Tinfoil. Tinfoil is very flimsy. This is definitely not a valid reason to think bmp is scum, although I’m sure you could find better reasons.

Good effort on that, though. Last note, NK spec is usually considered scummy, because it often comes from someone trying to direct that entirely WIFOM jumble into a pro-agenda direction. (For example, if you’re scum, and you killed aroodman to frame bmp, you’ll try to convince everyone that bmp is responsible for killing aroodman).
This post is deceptive. It’s true that nightkill speculation early on isn’t very accurate, and you should avoid too much of it early on as you could cause a bunch of chaos that scum can control and take advantage of. However, this doesn’t mean that scum don’t NK people who have accurate reads. It happens all the time, and anecdotally (although this may be outdated) not a lot of scum players WIFOM their night kills. Meaning, scum still night kill people who scumread them. If they’re good, they might think of disguising how they get rid of those players, but (and this is my personal opinion) WIFOM is fundamentally inefficient. And scum have to choose between PR hunting, getting rid of people who scumread them, and obscuring their identities by killing unexpected/entirely expected targets.

The reason that NK spec is considered bad is because it’s inaccurate, and it’s inaccurate because there are so many possible motives behind a singular NK. However, as the game progresses, and the POE narrows, NK spec becomes (in my opinion) much more effective. At some point, in some games, there can only be so many reasons why someone died. If you can recognize that situation, and synthesize various factors like multiple reads/multiple nightkills/POE/other reads together to form an educated guess, then looking at a dead player’s reads can be powerful. Honestly, my recommendation is to keep NK spec to yourself and note them down somewhere. Could eventually add up to something big.

My last nitpicky comment is that NK spec isn’t inherently scummy. Plenty of town do it, and I personally think in the right circumstances, it can be useful. NK spec is just often antitown. TL;DR, don’t scumread someone for NK spec but also don’t wildly NK spec unless you know what you’re doing.

Note: this post apparently triggered a lot of people because aroodman also wrote about this here and here.

Not cool. As an SE in a newbie game, that’s a super underhanded attempt at intimidation. ATE out of context is in no way close to being a scumtell. ATE isn’t even necessarily antitown; it’s annoying, yes. It’s also completely NAI.

Anyways, D2 was just ad333 bsing out of his mind to try to hang bmp, bmp freezing, and tikki pulling off his Buss So Hard You’ll Go To School plan. Which ended up working, somehow. Unlucky for town, tbh.

Also reminder that once it gets to D3/D4, be more careful with your votes, because it becomes that much easier for scum to quickhammer. Make sure you don’t accidentally hang someone, and scrutinize people who did go “Wait oops I didn’t mean to hammer, oh no what shall we do.”

Also, can we just talk about just how the reasons for hanging HelloWorld were just so flimsy? Suspected bussing is not a capital crime, please. Oh well. I have nothing else to say. Next lines are Hannah.

You should also always give the suspect a chance to respond. Patience is essential. There were days and days left, and you can always wait.

If I haven't already:
  • Lurking, not willing to consistently post and keep the game rolling along
  • Most recent posts were regarding her CL vote, which I called out for not having a good reason to back up the vote besides "sus"
  • She posted something about process of elimination but didn't give any reasons as to why people were eliminated from hangpool
  • Half of the posts are NAI from my perspective because they're vote posts or just talking about why they can't post as often as others
big_mathpro18 #708 wrote:
I get that the inactivity hang might not be the absolute best play here but I'm confident in my read on Felicityhe
Also, I could see ostriches/HW or maybe CL? as his partner if he flips scum

In newbie games (and also probably in most games unless you’re playing with experienced players) interactions are always a quick, easy, and arguably dirty way to get some clears ASAP. Remember that bussing is technically suboptimal, and remember to comb over interactions if you’re lucky enough to get a flip.

And remember to be careful with your votes.

And to not quickhammer. Every day shaved off the deadline was more depression for me. We have schedules, darn it.

Anyways, congratulations to everyone, and good luck in your future games!

4. ad333
From mathjay:

Your decision to not immediately claim cop after getting your red check was very, very good. The entire spec chat applauds you for that.

Here are some general thoughts on town PR play, if you ever find yourself facing a more complicated decision in the future: as an investigative PR, you should only claim either when you think you’re going to die the next night, or if you can see endgame. Endgame basically means a position where you have a (pretty much) guaranteed town win with the information that you have. Endgame doesn’t always have to be getting red checks on all mafia either; a mass claim, other important PR’s providing information or night actions, or you just having so many green checks you can POE the mafia can all be situations where you can claim as a cop or other PR. Another important part of playing PR’s well is softing; thinking about what could happen if you’re killed unexpectedly is something you can do. Like, if you’re a cop and you flip cop, good players might go through your previous reads and try to guess whether those reads were a result of an investigation. So it might be a good idea to say you strongly townread someone who you investigated as town. On the flip side, once you start playing with better players, mafia can pick up on these “soft” tells. If you do a complete 180 on someone you scumread because you copped them and they turned out to be town, that could be awkward. These mind games are one of the fun (?) parts of playing town PR.

Your reasons for your BMP scumread are decent, I thought, although tikki is right that aroodman is the NK even if BMP isn’t scum. In fact, aroodman gets NK’d on Night 1 so many times it’s become something of a meme. But nice wallposts. ISO’s are prodigally ineffective at actually finding scum but inordinately effective at convincing people that you’re right. Everybody hates to admit it, but overwhelming people with posts is a legitimate tactic.

Honestly, you analyzing interactions in this post is actually decent. Just know that people will actually put in effort to disguise their surface-level interactions once games get day-chat and players get better. The scum in this game sort of tried to too; you’ll notice in the mafia QT that tikki planned to (and somewhat awkwardly did) hard scumread bmp on Day 2.

In general, your reads were very good. You make decent use of POE and interactions, although you were initially caught off guard by tikki’s bussing. Your read on aroodman D1 was also understandable, seeing as his interactions with tikki would look really weird without context. But TL;DR, I think you’ll be fine with more experience and playing more games. No serious issues here!

Oh yea, another note on playing cop: once you get to D3/D4 ish where there are less people, it can be much more important to make sure you check people who aren't going to be NKed. This is obvious and you probably already realized this, but getting green checks can be just as powerful as red checks once you get to 5 or 3 people. Like imagine if you had checked ChompingLighting N2: going into D3, all you had to do was find a reason to clear one of Felicityhe/tikki and you basically won the game. It’s just something to think about. You did really well and we’re kidnapping you, so welcome to the team!

From mathjay:
Felicityhe #649 wrote:
Process of elimination. bmp, hw21, ad333 were mostly clear from my perspective. aroodman, tikki and snoozingnewt are somewhere between townlean and null, so that left ChompingLightning and Ostriches.
It’s really good that you’re using POE. It’s perfectly fine to use a combination of gut-townreads to sort of reorient yourself or if you’re having trouble coming up with reads! Just remember to reevaluate every once in a while, and you’ll be fine.

The reads you posted in post 752 are okay, but you also pretty much townread everyone! But it’s okay, it’s a nice start. Now, if you’re ever lost trying to make reads, it can be helpful to try to think from the other person’s perspective. Like, you said that ostriches88 was scummy because they were accusing people. From here, you can ask yourself, is he scum trying to frame people? Or is he town being paranoid? Why or why not? Starting from these questions, you can flesh out your read more and make it more solid. Don’t worry about your reads being wrong; even experienced players make reads that are totally off sometimes. The important thing is that you learn from what happened, and draw upon your experience in future games!

On another note, you were scumread by a lot of people early D1. That’s always unpleasant and it happens from time to time. My advice is, even if it’s hard, try to speak out more and talk about your thoughts! They don’t have to be big and fancy wallposts, just comments on another player or what’s happening in the thread. People often scumread players that they can’t really read, so if you make it easier for them to read you by talking about your thoughts more, you might be scumread less! This is a useful tip to remember, because whether you’re town or scum, being townread is part of your wincon! Like, even if your read’s are wrong as town, if you’re townread by other people and aren’t mishanged, that’s really helpful for town!

Good job, and best of luck in your future games!

[b]Day 2[/b]

[quote name="tikkitamatoa, #1142" url="/community/p21297810"]
ad, I agree with a little bit of your reasoning but nightkill speculation is entirely WIFOM. Meaning: someone else could go “if we kill aroodman it’ll look bad on bmp and we can get him hanged”. Basing reads off of the reads of the dead person is called a Tinfoil. Tinfoil is very flimsy. This is definitely not a valid reason to think bmp is scum, although I’m sure you could find better reasons. 

Good effort on that, though. Last note, NK spec is usually considered scummy, because it often comes from someone trying to direct that entirely WIFOM jumble into a pro-agenda direction. (For example, if you’re scum, and you killed aroodman to frame bmp, you’ll try to convince everyone that bmp is responsible for killing aroodman). 
[/quote]
This post is deceptive. It’s true that nightkill speculation [i]early on[/i] isn’t very accurate, and you should avoid too much of it early on as you could cause a bunch of chaos that scum can control and take advantage of. However, this doesn’t mean that scum don’t NK people who have accurate reads. It happens all the time, and anecdotally (although this may be outdated) not a lot of scum players WIFOM their night kills. Meaning, scum still night kill people who scumread them. If they’re good, they might think of disguising how they get rid of those players, but (and this is my personal opinion) WIFOM is fundamentally inefficient. And scum have to choose between PR hunting, getting rid of people who scumread them, and obscuring their identities by killing unexpected/entirely expected targets. 

The reason that NK spec is considered bad is because it’s inaccurate, and it’s inaccurate because there are so many possible motives behind a singular NK. However, as the game progresses, and the POE narrows, NK spec becomes (in my opinion) much more effective. At some point, in some games, there can only be so many reasons why someone died. If you can recognize that situation, and synthesize various factors like multiple reads/multiple nightkills/POE/other reads together to form an educated guess, then looking at a dead player’s reads can be powerful. Honestly, my recommendation is to keep NK spec to yourself and note them down somewhere. Could eventually add up to something big. 

My last nitpicky comment is that NK spec isn’t inherently scummy. Plenty of town do it, and I personally think in the right circumstances, it can be useful. NK spec is just often [i]antitown[/i]. TL;DR, don’t scumread someone for NK spec but also don’t wildly NK spec unless you know what you’re doing. 

Note: this post apparently triggered a lot of people because aroodman also wrote about this [url=https://aops.com/community/p21624137]here[/url] and [url=https://aops.com/community/p21624313]here[/url].

[hide=4. ad333]
[b]From mathjay:[/b]

Your decision to not immediately claim cop after getting your red check was very, very good. The entire spec chat applauds you for that. 

Here are some general thoughts on town PR play, if you ever find yourself facing a more complicated decision in the future: as an investigative PR, you should only claim either when you think you’re going to die the next night, or if you can see endgame. Endgame basically means a position where you have a (pretty much) guaranteed town win with the information that you have. Endgame doesn’t always have to be getting red checks on all mafia either; a mass claim, other important PR’s providing information or night actions, or you just having so many green checks you can POE the mafia can all be situations where you can claim as a cop or other PR. Another important part of playing PR’s well is softing; thinking about what could happen if you’re killed unexpectedly is something you can do. Like, if you’re a cop and you flip cop, good players might go through your previous reads and try to guess whether those reads were a result of an investigation. So it might be a good idea to say you strongly townread someone who you investigated as town. On the flip side, once you start playing with better players, mafia can pick up on these “soft” tells. If you do a complete 180 on someone you scumread because you copped them and they turned out to be town, that could be awkward. These mind games are one of the fun (?) parts of playing town PR. 

[url=https://aops.com/community/p21296663]Your reasons[/url] for your BMP scumread are decent, I thought, although tikki is right that aroodman is the NK even if BMP isn’t scum. In fact, aroodman gets NK’d on Night 1 so many times it’s become something of a meme. But nice wallposts. ISO’s are prodigally ineffective at actually finding scum but inordinately effective at convincing people that you’re right. Everybody hates to admit it, but overwhelming people with posts is a legitimate tactic. 

Honestly, you analyzing interactions [url=https://aops.com/community/p21311875]in this post[/url] is actually decent. Just know that people will actually put in effort to disguise their surface-level interactions once games get day-chat and players get better. The scum in this game sort of tried to too; you’ll notice in the mafia QT that tikki planned to (and somewhat awkwardly did) hard scumread bmp on Day 2. 

In general, your reads were very good. You make decent use of POE and interactions, although you were initially caught off guard by tikki’s bussing. Your read on aroodman D1 was also understandable, seeing as his interactions with tikki would look really weird without context. But TL;DR, I think you’ll be fine with more experience and playing more games. No serious issues here!

Oh yea, another note on playing cop: once you get to D3/D4 ish where there are less people, it can be much more important to make sure you check people who aren't going to be NKed. This is obvious and you probably already realized this, but getting green checks can be just as powerful as red checks once you get to 5 or 3 people. Like imagine if you had checked ChompingLighting N2: going into D3, all you had to do was find a reason to clear one of Felicityhe/tikki and you basically won the game. It’s just something to think about. You did really well and we’re kidnapping you, so welcome to the team!

[/hide]

[quote name="Felicityhe #649" url="/community/p21049673"]
Process of elimination. bmp, hw21, ad333 were mostly clear from my perspective. aroodman, tikki and snoozingnewt are somewhere between townlean and null, so that left ChompingLightning and Ostriches.

It’s really good that you’re using POE. It’s perfectly fine to use a combination of gut-townreads to sort of reorient yourself or if you’re having trouble coming up with reads! Just remember to reevaluate every once in a while, and you’ll be fine.

The reads you posted in [url=https://aops.com/community/p21082336]post 752[/url] are okay, but you also pretty much townread everyone! But it’s okay, it’s a nice start. Now, if you’re ever lost trying to make reads, it can be helpful to try to think from the other person’s perspective. Like, you said that ostriches88 was scummy because they were accusing people. From here, you can ask yourself, is he scum trying to frame people? Or is he town being paranoid? Why or why not? Starting from these questions, you can flesh out your read more and make it more solid. Don’t worry about your reads being wrong; even experienced players make reads that are totally off sometimes. The important thing is that you learn from what happened, and draw upon your experience in future games! 

On another note, you were scumread by a lot of people early D1. That’s always unpleasant and it happens from time to time. My advice is, even if it’s hard, try to speak out more and talk about your thoughts! They don’t have to be big and fancy wallposts, just comments on another player or what’s happening in the thread. People often scumread players that they can’t really read, so if you make it easier for them to read you by talking about your thoughts more, you might be scumread less! This is a useful tip to remember, because whether you’re town or scum, being townread is part of your wincon! Like, even if your read’s are wrong as town, if you’re townread by other people and aren’t mishanged, that’s really helpful for town! 

Good job, and best of luck in your future games!
[/hide]

So, what you can do here is read people off of their motivations! Like, when you read their posts, you can try to think about what it does. Imagine you see someone scumread someone else. Why do you think they scumread them? Is their reason valid, or does it seem made up? Is it opportunist, like trying to get an easy hang? Do you scumread the other person too? Do you think it’s town genuinely trying to scumhunt, or scum trying to fake a read?

Can you think of any reasons why Felicityhe could’ve done what they did as town? And if you’re having difficulties reading someone on their own, remember that you can clear them in other ways, such as through POE and interactions later on.
